result.markdown:
--- title: "Choosing the Cell Resolution" jupyter: python3 --- ```{=html} ``` # The resolution problem The scale-decomposition bound on preferential-sampling bias (Prop. *scale-decomposition* of the methodology) reads, on an equal-measure partition $\mathcal{P}_K$ with $K=K_{\mathrm{lon}}K_{\mathrm{lat}}K_t$, $$ \big|B_{\mathrm{samp}}\big| \;\le\;\sigma_{w^{\star}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\star}} \;+\;\sigma_{w^{\perp}}\,\sigma_{Y^{\perp}} , $$ with field factors $\sigma_{Y^\star}=\|y^\star\|_{L^2(\nu)}$ (resolved, between-cell) and $\sigma_{Y^\perp}=\|y^\perp\|_{L^2(\nu)}$ (within-cell), where $y^\star=\mathsf{P}_K(y-\mu)$, $y^\perp=\mathsf{P}_K^{\perp}(y-\mu)$ are the cell-averaging / within-cell projections of the OHC field $y\in L^2(\nu)$ and $\nu$ is the normalized area$\times$time reference measure on $\mathcal{X}=A\times\mathcal{T}$. The one factor that cannot be estimated from a sparse point pattern, $\sigma_{w^\perp}$, enters only through the product $\sigma_{w^\perp}\sigma_{Y^\perp}$; it is therefore bias-irrelevant to the extent the field is constant within cells. **So the cell scale must make the fraction of field variance below it, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$, small** — while keeping the mean occupancy $N/K$ (in practice $F/K$, with $F$ the number of distinct floats) above one. The field factors, unlike $\sigma_{w^\star}$, are computable directly on the dense GLORYS field over the NAC box (lon $[-68,-62]$, lat $[36,40]$, 2020–2022 daily at $1/12^\circ$), which resolves $\sigma_{Y^\star},\sigma_{Y^\perp},\sigma_Y$ exactly. This report fixes the resolution in two logically separate steps — **spatial** ($K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}$) and **temporal** ($K_t$) — and, importantly, the spatial choice can be made *independently of seasonality* (see below), so we settle it first. ::: {#setup .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="Setup — run (or load cached) R analyses"} import sys, os sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code")) import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import resolution as R DEPTHS = ["700", "2000"] D = "../data/leakage" TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV = f"{D}/leakage_time.csv", f"{D}/leakage_full.csv", f"{D}/leakage_spatial.csv" VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF = f"{D}/variogram_ranges.csv", f"{D}/variogram_curves.csv", f"{D}/variogram_acf.csv" if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV)): R.run_leakage_curves(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV, depths=DEPTHS) if not all(os.path.exists(p) for p in (VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF)): R.run_variogram(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF, depths=DEPTHS) time_df, full_df, spatial_df = R.load_tables(TIME_CSV, FULL_CSV, SPATIAL_CSV) vg_ranges, vg_curves, vg_acf = R.load_tables(VG_RANGES, VG_CURVES, VG_ACF) ``` ::: # Spatial resolution {#sec-spatial} ::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"} **This section is free of seasonality.** The quantities below are *per-snapshot spatial* variances. A seasonal cycle that is (near) spatially uniform shifts every location on a given day by the same amount, and an additive constant does not change that day's spatial variance. So the spatial within-cell variance — and hence the choice of $K_{\mathrm{lon}},K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ — is settled on raw OHC without any deseasonalizing. Seasonality enters only the temporal scale, handled separately in @sec-temporal. ::: The most direct test of a spatial grid is empirical: partition each daily snapshot into $K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ equal-measure (lon $\times \sin$lat) cells, take the $\cos(\mathrm{lat})$ area-weighted within-cell spatial variance and total spatial variance, and average over snapshots (`spatial_leakage` in `code/leakage_curve.R`, eq. *sigmaYperp-hat* evaluated on the native $1/12^\circ$ grid). Their ratio is the **fraction of spatial field variance below the cell scale**, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$ — exactly the quantity the bound needs small. As cells shrink it must fall to zero; the question is how small it already is at the coarsest cell the floats can support, $s_F\approx 76$ km. ::: {#cell-spatial-fraction .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](resolution_files/figure-html/spatial-fraction-output-2.png){#spatial-fraction width=662 height=431} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: ::: {#spatial-table .cell execution_count=4}
depth target_km K_lon K_lat n_cells cell (km) var frac (perp^2/Y^2) sd ratio (perp/Y)
0-700 m 200 3 2 6 197.069 0.360 0.600
0-700 m 150 3 3 9 160.906 0.266 0.515
0-700 m 100 5 4 20 107.939 0.157 0.396
0-700 m 75 7 6 42 74.485 0.086 0.293
0-700 m 50 10 9 90 50.883 0.044 0.210
0-700 m 35 15 13 195 34.568 0.021 0.147

0-700 m 18 29 25 725 17.928 0.005 0.072
0-700 m 12 44 37 1628 11.964 0.002 0.043
0-2000 m 200 3 2 6 197.069 0.324 0.570
0-2000 m 150 3 3 9 160.906 0.238 0.487
0-2000 m 100 5 4 20 107.939 0.135 0.367
0-2000 m 75 7 6 42 74.485 0.072 0.268
0-2000 m 50 10 9 90 50.883 0.036 0.189
0-2000 m 35 15 13 195 34.568 0.017 0.131
0-2000 m 25 21 18 378 24.828 0.009 0.093
0-2000 m 18 29 25 725 17.928 0.004 0.064
0-2000 m 12 44 37 1628 11.964 0.001 0.038
::: Reading off the curve at $s_F\approx76$ km fixes the spatial grid: the smallest $K_{\mathrm{lon}}\times K_{\mathrm{lat}}$ whose cell size sits at or below the decorrelation scale while keeping the residual fraction acceptably small. The paper's operating grid $K_{\mathrm{lon}}=6,\ K_{\mathrm{lat}}=5$ ($\Delta x\approx88$ km, $\Delta y\approx89$ km) is read directly from this curve. ## Decorrelation length (directional variogram) The variogram corroborates the cell-size curve above and, unlike it, exposes the **anisotropy** that the front imposes. Within each snapshot we form grid-point pairs (subsampled), classify each by orientation, and accumulate the semivariance $\tfrac12(y_i-y_j)^2$ into zonal ($|\theta|<30^\circ$), meridional ($|\theta|>60^\circ$) and omnidirectional bins, pooled over snapshots — again a per-snapshot construction, so again seasonality-free. It *must* be directional: correlation is long along the current and short across it, and an isotropic average would hide the short across-front scale, which is the binding one. (Raw OHC has a per-snapshot planar trend removed so the standing Gulf-Stream gradient does not render the variogram non-stationary; the deseasonalized panel removes the per-snapshot mean.) ::: {#variograms .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.panel-tabset} ### 0-700 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/variograms-output-2.png){#variograms-1 width=1143 height=418}
### 0-2000 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/variograms-output-4.png){#variograms-2 width=1143 height=418}
::: ::: The binding relevance scale is the **shorter, meridional (across-front)** range: $\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ is controlled by field variation in *every* within-cell direction, so the shortest one governs. The methodology's verdict is $L_{\mathrm{rel}}\gtrsim s_F=\sqrt{|A|/F}\approx76$ km (eq. *verdict*): the field's shortest decorrelation length must be no finer than the scale the floats can resolve. Where it holds, cells at the sampling scale sit within a decorrelation length and $\sigma_{Y^\perp}$ is small; where it fails, a band of bias-relevant structure is unresolved and the residual term must be carried rather than assumed away. ::: {#ranges-table .cell execution_count=6}
depth field direction sill range 1/e (km) range exp (km)
0-700 m raw omni 43.513 85.2 69.4
0-700 m raw zonal 43.513 100.0 118.1
0-700 m raw merid 43.513 76.1 50.3
0-700 m deseas omni 28.389 80.4 67.8
0-700 m deseas zonal 28.389 84.5 88.6
0-700 m deseas merid 28.389 76.3 51.8
0-2000 m raw omni 90.477 89.0 72.7

::: # Temporal resolution and seasonality {#sec-temporal} ::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"} **This section is where seasonality lives.** The number of time bins $K_t$ is governed by temporal structure of the regional-mean OHC series, which is dominated by the seasonal cycle; the spatial choice of @sec-spatial does not depend on any of it. ::: Collapsing space to the area-weighted regional mean $\bar Y(t)$ (the $K_{\mathrm{lon}}=K_{\mathrm{lat}}=1$ special case of $\mathcal{P}_K$), we bin the 2020–2022 series into $K_t$ equal-duration bins and report the fraction of its temporal variance left within bins, $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$, for the **raw** series and for a **deseasonalized** series (minus a per-cell, per-calendar-month GLORYS climatology — an Argo-independent baseline). The gap between them is the seasonal cycle: the raw fraction stays large until many time bins resolve the seasonal swing, whereas deseasonalizing removes it up front so even a coarse $K_t$ leaks little. ::: {#temporal-fraction .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.panel-tabset} ## 0-700 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/temporal-fraction-output-2.png){#temporal-fraction-1 width=664 height=431}
## 0-2000 m
![](resolution_files/figure-html/temporal-fraction-output-4.png){#temporal-fraction-2 width=664 height=431}
::: ::: The lagged autocorrelation of the deseasonalized regional mean gives the residual (non-seasonal) temporal decorrelation scale; its $e$-folding lag, together with the seasonal amplitude above, sets $K_t$. ::: {#cell-temporal-acf .cell execution_count=8} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](resolution_files/figure-html/temporal-acf-output-2.png){#temporal-acf width=645 height=412} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ```
``` ::: ::: ::: {#temporal-table .cell execution_count=9}
depth K_t raw deseas
0-700 m 3 0.968 0.941
0-700 m 6 0.528 0.728
0-700 m 12 0.483 0.657
0-700 m 24 0.225 0.327
0-2000 m 3 0.936 0.905
0-2000 m 6 0.608 0.733
0-2000 m 12 0.564 0.668
0-2000 m 24 0.294 0.363
::: # Operating partition {#sec-operating} On the full partition $\mathcal{P}_K$ at the operating spatial grid ($K_{\mathrm{lon}}=6,\ K_{\mathrm{lat}}=5$), the combined field variance splits by the Pythagorean identity $\sigma_Y^2=\sigma_{Y^\star}^2+\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2$ (eq. *sigmaYK-hat / sigmaYperp-hat*, native grid aggregated to equal-measure cells). The residual diagnostic there, across $K_t$: ::: {#operating-checks .cell execution_count=10} Pythagorean identity holds by construction (max residual $2.8e-14$); the equal-measure quadrature reconstructs the direct $\cos(\mathrm{lat})$-weighted field variance to within **0.8%** (largest relative gap across resolutions), bounding the error of treating the discrete cells as exactly equal-measure. Combined space$\times$time variance fraction $\sigma_{Y^\perp}^2/\sigma_Y^2$:
